turns.content: I'm trying to implement a simple chat server with a client using derive macros for the session protocol, but I get an error saying that `impl Serialize` is not implemented. What went wrong?

That error usually means you're trying to use standard serialization traits on values that need linear usage instead of reusable ones. Think of it like a single-use cup: if you try to pour water into it twice, the first attempt fails just as your code rejects the value because it's been consumed. In session types, each message must be sent exactly once and then discarded; if you reuse the same `Box<dyn Any>` or generic trait object for multiple parties in a loop without tracking consumption via linear traits like `LinearSend` or `LinearReceive`, the compiler will refuse to let you compile the protocol because it assumes messages can only exist where they are used. You need to encode your handshake as a sequence of distinct operations where each step consumes its specific channel endpoint before moving to the next, rather than reusing generic types that allow arbitrary duplication.
Ah okay so I can't just reuse the same trait object for both the client and server? But what if I want my protocol to support multiple concurrent sessions?

You absolutely can, but you need to ensure each session has its own distinct linear channel endpoints that are never shared. Imagine having a dedicated cup for every conversation; even though they all look the same externally, internally they are separate instances of `LinearSend<T>` and `LinearReceive<T>` tied to specific file descriptors or socket handles in your runtime. The key is that your type system must guarantee no two cups are ever mixed into one stream unless you explicitly create a new linear channel for each connection via something like `tokio::net::TcpStream` or `std::net::TcpListener`, and then attach those raw endpoints to separate session types so the compiler knows they won't conflict. If you're using crates like `linear-derive` or implementing your own macro-based protocol encoding, make sure every generated type explicitly marks its fields as linear so that once a message is sent on one channel, it physically cannot be reused on another without breaking the safety guarantee.

I'm trying to build a data pipeline tool again, but I keep hitting edge cases where the sequence of operations matters more than just what data flows through. It feels like when you use async/await in Tokio, the order of calls defines the behavior, but here I need something stricter so I can't accidentally duplicate or drop items mid-stream.

That's exactly why session types are useful; they let you encode communication protocols directly in your type system using linear types simulation. Instead of relying on runtime checks, the compiler ensures that every message sent is consumed and never duplicated by enforcing correct-by-construction rules on your channels.

I've seen something similar with Rust's std::sync::mpsc channel where if you clone a sender or receiver, it breaks the logic. But I'm worried about complex protocols like state machines where the type alone isn't enough.

You're right to worry because standard channels don't capture protocol constraints; for that you need a crate like session-typed-rust or similar libraries that implement linear types simulation. Think of it like an immutable borrow checker but extended so that the lifecycle of each message is tracked, ensuring your tool never deviates from the intended communication pattern.
Wait, so if I define my protocol using session-typed-rust, the compiler will catch any mismatch before runtime? That means I can't accidentally drop a message or send twice?

Exactly; by encoding your communication protocol in the type system with linear types simulation, the compiler enforces that every action is valid and no message is duplicated or lost. You get correct-by-construction guarantees so your automated tool behaves predictably without manual testing for ordering issues.

I'm trying to build an automation script that exchanges a JSON payload with an external API, but I keep getting runtime errors where the message format drifts. Is there a way to encode this communication protocol directly into the Rust type system so the compiler catches mismatches before execution?

That's exactly what session types are designed for, but in Rust we simulate them using linear types and traits like Drop and Send instead of true linear logic. You can define a trait that enforces the sequence of actions your tool must perform, such as sending a request then receiving a response, so the type checker ensures no messages are lost or duplicated.

Wait, I thought session types meant we just need to declare a protocol and let Rust handle it automatically without any manual traits or checks. Can't I just write a struct with a Session field and have the compiler do all the work?

That's a common assumption, but linear types require explicit constraints on how values are used because Rust doesn't have true linearity by default. If you omit the necessary trait bounds or don't ensure each message is consumed exactly once via traits like Drop and Send, your type system won't catch the drift until runtime when serde_json::Value deserialization fails.
